annotations.label: Sequence (informal)

anchor.snippet: a sequence is a collection of objects possibly with repetition

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Seq α` models a possibly-finite-or-infinite ordered sequence (with `Stream'`/`List` for the purely infinite/finite cases).

annotations.label: Indexed family

anchor.snippet: can be generalized to an indexed family

annotations.note: Indexed families are represented pervasively as plain dependent functions `ι → α` / `(i : ι) → π i`, but there is no dedicated `IndexedFamily` definition.

annotations.label: Sequence of letters (M,A,R,Y)

anchor.snippet: is a sequence of letters with the letter

annotations.note: A specific illustrative example with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Length of a finite sequence

anchor.snippet: The length of a finite sequence is defined as the number of elements

annotations.note: Length of a finite sequence is `List.length` (a Lean-core function with extensive Mathlib API).

annotations.label: Position / index of an element

anchor.snippet: The position of an element in a sequence is its rank or index

annotations.note: Position is just the function argument / list index (`List.get`); no dedicated notion is formalized.

annotations.label: Strings, words, lists (computing)

anchor.snippet: finite sequences are usually called strings

annotations.note: Finite sequences/words are `FreeMonoid α := List α` (with `List`/`String` as the core types).

annotations.label: Empty sequence

anchor.snippet: is included in most notions of sequence

annotations.note: The empty finite sequence is `List.nil` / `[]` (Lean core).

annotations.label: Listing notation

anchor.snippet: One way to specify a sequence is to list all its elements

annotations.note: A notational device for specifying sequences, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Prime number

anchor.snippet: A prime number is a natural numbers greater than 1

annotations.note: `Nat.Prime p` is the standard definition of a prime natural number.

annotations.label: Sequence of prime numbers

anchor.snippet: Listing the prime numbers in their natural order gives the sequence

annotations.note: `Nat.nth Nat.Prime` enumerates the primes in order, with lemmas like `Nat.nth_prime_zero_eq_two`.

annotations.label: Fibonacci numbers

anchor.snippet: The Fibonacci numbers are a sequence for which each element is the sum of the previous two elements

annotations.note: `Nat.fib` is the Fibonacci sequence.

annotations.label: Rational sequence approaching 1

anchor.snippet: for instance, approaches the number 1

annotations.note: A specific illustrative example with no dedicated declaration.

annotations.label: Every real is a limit of rationals

anchor.snippet: every real number can be written as the limit of a sequence of rational numbers

annotations.note: `Rat.denseRange_cast` gives density of ℚ (hence every real is a rational limit) for any linear ordered archimedean field.

annotations.label: Decimal digits of a real number

anchor.snippet: A related type of sequence consists of the decimal digits of a real number

annotations.note: `Real.digits x b` is the base-`b` digit sequence of a real number (decimal is `b = 10`).

annotations.label: Monomial basis sequence

anchor.snippet: the monomial basis for polynomials of a single variable forms the sequence

annotations.note: `Polynomial.basisMonomials` is the basis `Xⁿ` of `R[X]` (`coe_basisMonomials` gives `fun s => monomial s 1`).

annotations.label: OEIS

anchor.snippet: comprises a large list of examples of integer sequences

annotations.note: An external database, not a mathematical object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Index and index set

anchor.snippet: The variable n is called an index

annotations.note: The index/index set is just the domain of the function; no dedicated notion is defined.

annotations.label: Bi-infinite sequence (indexing)

anchor.snippet: A bi-infinite sequence is a sequence indexed by

annotations.note: A ℤ-indexed sequence is representable as `ℤ → α` but has no dedicated definition.

annotations.label: Recurrence relation

anchor.snippet: one needs a rule, called recurrence relation to construct each element

annotations.note: The general notion of recurrence relation is not a named concept (only special cases like `LinearRecurrence` are formalized).

annotations.label: Fibonacci by recurrence

anchor.snippet: The Fibonacci sequence is a simple classical example

annotations.note: `Nat.fib_add_two` proves `fib (n+2) = fib n + fib (n+1)`, the Fibonacci recurrence.

annotations.label: Recamán's sequence

anchor.snippet: A complicated example of a sequence defined by a recurrence relation is Recamán's sequence

annotations.note: Recamán's sequence is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear recurrence with constant coefficients

anchor.snippet: A linear recurrence with constant coefficients is a recurrence relation of the form

annotations.note: `LinearRecurrence` is the structure of a linear recurrence with constant coefficients.

annotations.label: Holonomic sequence

anchor.snippet: A holonomic sequence is a sequence defined by a recurrence relation of the form

annotations.note: Holonomic (P-recursive) sequences are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Not all sequences satisfy a recurrence

anchor.snippet: Not all sequences can be specified by a recurrence relation

annotations.note: A meta-mathematical remark with no formal counterpart.

annotations.label: Sequence (formal, as a function)

anchor.snippet: a sequence can be defined as a function whose domain is an interval of integers

annotations.note: `Stream' α := ℕ → α` defines a sequence as a function with index-set domain ℕ.

annotations.label: Length of a sequence

anchor.snippet: The length of a sequence is defined as the number of terms in the sequence

annotations.note: Length of a finite sequence is `List.length` (Lean core).

annotations.label: Finite sequence / n-tuple

anchor.snippet: A sequence of a finite length is a finite sequence

annotations.note: Finite sequences are `List α`; n-tuples are `Fin n → α` (core types with Mathlib API).

annotations.label: Infinite and bi-infinite sequences

anchor.snippet: infinite sequence refers to a sequence that is infinite in one direction

annotations.note: One-directional infinite sequences are `Stream' α := ℕ → α`, but bi-infinite (ℤ-indexed) sequences have no dedicated definition.

annotations.label: Monotonically increasing

anchor.snippet: monotonically increasing if each term is greater than or equal to the one before it

annotations.note: `Monotone` captures (weakly) increasing functions/sequences.

annotations.label: Monotonically decreasing

anchor.snippet: monotonically decreasing if each consecutive term is less than or equal to the previous one

annotations.note: `Antitone` captures (weakly) decreasing functions/sequences.

annotations.label: Monotone sequence

anchor.snippet: it is called a monotone sequence

annotations.note: A monotone sequence is `Monotone` (or `Antitone`).

annotations.label: Nondecreasing / nonincreasing

anchor.snippet: The terms nondecreasing and nonincreasing are often used in place

annotations.note: Nondecreasing is `Monotone` and nonincreasing is `Antitone`.

annotations.label: Bounded from above

anchor.snippet: the sequence is said to be bounded from above

annotations.note: Bounded above is `BddAbove (Set.range u)`.

annotations.label: Upper bound

anchor.snippet: Any such M is called an upper bound

annotations.note: `upperBounds s` is the set of upper bounds of `s`.

annotations.label: Bounded from below / lower bound

anchor.snippet: the sequence is bounded from below and any such m is called a lower bound

annotations.note: Bounded below is `BddBelow (Set.range u)` and lower bounds form `lowerBounds`.

annotations.label: Bounded sequence

anchor.snippet: If a sequence is both bounded from above and bounded from below

annotations.note: A bounded sequence has bounded range, i.e. `Bornology.IsBounded (Set.range u)` (equivalently `BddAbove` ∧ `BddBelow`).

annotations.label: Subsequence (informal)

anchor.snippet: A subsequence of a given sequence is a sequence formed from the given sequence by deleting some of the elements

annotations.note: Subsequences are encoded as `u ∘ φ` with `StrictMono φ : ℕ → ℕ`, but no dedicated `Subsequence` object exists.

annotations.label: Subsequence (formal)

anchor.snippet: Formally, a subsequence of the sequence

annotations.note: The formal `a_{n_k}` is realized as `a ∘ φ` for `StrictMono φ`, used throughout Mathlib but without its own definition.

annotations.label: Integer sequence

anchor.snippet: An integer sequence is a sequence whose terms are integers

annotations.note: Just a function `ℕ → ℤ`; no dedicated definition.

annotations.label: Polynomial sequence

anchor.snippet: A polynomial sequence is a sequence whose terms are polynomials

annotations.note: Just a function `ℕ → R[X]`; no dedicated definition of a polynomial sequence.

annotations.label: Multiplicative sequence

anchor.snippet: A positive integer sequence is sometimes called multiplicative

annotations.note: `ArithmeticFunction.IsMultiplicative` is exactly `f(mn)=f(m)f(n)` for coprime `m,n` (with `f 1 = 1`).

annotations.label: Binary sequence

anchor.snippet: A binary sequence is a sequence whose terms have one of two discrete values

annotations.note: Binary sequences `ℕ → Bool` are studied (as the Cantor space) but have no dedicated definition.

annotations.label: Convergent sequence / limit

anchor.snippet: it converges to a particular value known as the limit

annotations.note: Convergence of a sequence to a limit is `Filter.Tendsto u atTop (𝓝 l)`.

annotations.label: Divergent sequence

anchor.snippet: A sequence that does not converge is divergent

annotations.note: Divergence is just the negation of convergence; no dedicated predicate is defined.

annotations.label: Limit (informal)

anchor.snippet: a sequence has a limit if the elements of the sequence become closer and closer

annotations.note: The limit notion is `Filter.Tendsto` along `atTop` to the neighborhood filter.

annotations.label: Convergent and divergent examples

anchor.snippet: shown to the right converges to the value 0

annotations.note: Specific illustrative examples with no dedicated declarations.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of the limit

anchor.snippet: then the value it converges to is unique

annotations.note: `tendsto_nhds_unique` proves limits are unique in Hausdorff (hence metric) spaces.

annotations.label: Convergence (epsilon-N)

anchor.snippet: there exists a natural number

annotations.note: `Metric.tendsto_atTop` is the ε–N characterization of sequence convergence.

annotations.label: Convergence in complex / metric spaces

anchor.snippet: this last formula can still be used to define convergence

annotations.note: `Metric.tendsto_atTop` defines convergence in any (pseudo)metric space, including ℂ.

annotations.label: Limit laws

anchor.snippet: then the following limits exist, and can be computed as follows

annotations.note: Limit laws are `Filter.Tendsto.add`, `Filter.Tendsto.mul`, etc., for topological algebraic structures.

annotations.label: Limits preserve order

anchor.snippet: greater than some

annotations.note: `le_of_tendsto_of_tendsto` shows order is preserved in the limit.

annotations.label: Squeeze theorem

anchor.snippet: Squeeze theorem

annotations.note: `tendsto_of_tendsto_of_tendsto_of_le_of_le` is the squeeze/sandwich theorem.

annotations.label: Monotone bounded sequences converge

anchor.snippet: If a sequence is bounded and monotonic then it is convergent

annotations.note: `tendsto_atTop_ciSup` proves a monotone, bounded-above sequence converges to its supremum.

annotations.label: Convergence via subsequences

anchor.snippet: all of its subsequences are convergent

annotations.note: `tendsto_of_subseq_tendsto`: if every subsequence has a sub-subsequence tending to `a`, the sequence tends to `a`.

annotations.label: Cauchy sequence

anchor.snippet: A Cauchy sequence is a sequence whose terms become arbitrarily close together

annotations.note: `CauchySeq` defines Cauchy sequences (with `IsCauSeq` for ordered fields).

annotations.label: Cauchy characterization of convergence

anchor.snippet: A sequence of real numbers is convergent (in the reals) if and only if it is Cauchy

annotations.note: `cauchySeq_tendsto_of_complete` gives Cauchy ⟹ convergent in a complete space (ℝ is a `CompleteSpace`), the converse being `Filter.Tendsto.cauchySeq`.

annotations.label: Rational Cauchy sequence with no rational limit

anchor.snippet: there are Cauchy sequences of rational numbers that are not convergent in the rationals

annotations.note: The incompleteness of ℚ is implicit in the real construction but not stated as a standalone example here.

annotations.label: Complete metric space

anchor.snippet: are called complete metric spaces

annotations.note: `CompleteSpace` is the typeclass for spaces where every Cauchy filter converges.

annotations.label: Divergence to infinity

anchor.snippet: we say that the sequence diverges , or that it converges to infinity

annotations.note: Divergence to +∞ is `Filter.Tendsto u atTop atTop`.

annotations.label: Divergence to negative infinity

anchor.snippet: say that the sequence diverges or converges to negative infinity

annotations.note: Divergence to −∞ is `Filter.Tendsto u atTop atBot`.

annotations.label: Series

anchor.snippet: A series is, informally speaking, the sum of the terms of a sequence

annotations.note: Infinite sums are `tsum` (`∑'`) / `HasSum`, the (unordered) series of a sequence.

annotations.label: Partial sums

anchor.snippet: The partial sums of a series are the expressions resulting from replacing the infinity symbol

annotations.note: Partial sums are `∑ i ∈ Finset.range n, a i`, the finite sums on which `HasSum` is built.

annotations.label: Convergent series / value

anchor.snippet: is called the value of the series

annotations.note: `HasSum f a` says the series converges with value `a` (also recoverable as `tsum`).

annotations.label: Compactness via sequential compactness

anchor.snippet: A metric space is compact exactly when it is sequentially compact

annotations.note: `isCompact_iff_isSeqCompact` proves compactness ⟺ sequential compactness (in suitable uniform/metric settings).

annotations.label: Sequential characterization of continuity

anchor.snippet: is continuous exactly when it takes convergent sequences to convergent sequences

annotations.note: `continuous_iff_seqContinuous` gives continuity ⟺ sequential continuity for sequential (e.g. metric) spaces.

annotations.label: Sequential characterization of connectedness

anchor.snippet: A metric space is a connected space if and only if

annotations.note: No sequential characterization of connectedness is formalized (only `ConnectedSpace` itself).

annotations.label: Separability via dense sequence

anchor.snippet: A topological space is separable exactly when there is a dense sequence of points

annotations.note: `exists_dense_seq` produces a dense sequence in a (nonempty) `SeparableSpace`, capturing the dense-sequence characterization.

annotations.label: Generalization to nets and filters

anchor.snippet: Sequences can be generalized to nets or filters

annotations.note: Filters are formalized as `Filter`; Mathlib uses filters rather than nets as the primary generalization.

annotations.label: Topological product

anchor.snippet: The topological product of a sequence of topological spaces is the cartesian product

annotations.note: `Pi.topologicalSpace` is the product topology on the dependent cartesian product `∀ i, Y i`.

annotations.label: Canonical projections

anchor.snippet: The canonical projections are the maps

annotations.note: `continuous_apply` shows each projection `fun f => f i` from a product is continuous.

annotations.label: Product topology (coarsest topology)

anchor.snippet: the product topology on X is defined to be the coarsest topology

annotations.note: `Pi.topologicalSpace` is defined as `⨅ i, induced (· i) (t i)`, the coarsest topology making all projections continuous.

annotations.label: Sequence with shifted starting index

anchor.snippet: A sequence may start with an index different from 1 or 0

annotations.note: Mathlib sequences are ℕ-indexed; index shifting is handled ad hoc with no dedicated declaration.

annotations.label: Sequence space

anchor.snippet: A sequence space is a vector space whose elements are infinite sequences

annotations.note: Specific sequence spaces like `lp` exist, but there is no general `SequenceSpace` definition.

annotations.label: ℓp spaces

anchor.snippet: are the ℓ p spaces, consisting of the p -power summable sequences

annotations.note: `lp E p` is the space of `p`-power summable sequences/families.

annotations.label: Spaces c and c0

anchor.snippet: convergent sequences or null sequences form sequence spaces

annotations.note: The spaces `c` (convergent) and `c₀` (null sequences) are not defined as named sequence spaces in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sequences as vectors

anchor.snippet: Sequences over a field may also be viewed as vectors in a vector space

annotations.note: `Pi.module` gives the module/vector-space structure on function spaces such as `ℕ → K`.

annotations.label: Free monoid (Kleene star)

anchor.snippet: is a monoid containing all the finite sequences

annotations.note: `FreeMonoid α := List α` is the free monoid (Kleene star `A*`) of finite sequences.

annotations.label: Free semigroup

anchor.snippet: The free semigroup A + is the subsemigroup of A *

annotations.note: `FreeSemigroup α` is the free semigroup (nonempty finite sequences `A⁺`).

annotations.label: Exact sequence

anchor.snippet: is called exact , if the image (or range ) of each homomorphism is equal to the kernel of the next

annotations.note: `Function.Exact f g` says `range f = ker g`, with `CategoryTheory.ComposableArrows.Exact` for longer sequences.

annotations.label: Spectral sequence

anchor.snippet: a spectral sequence is a means of computing homology groups by taking successive approximations

annotations.note: Spectral sequences are not formalized in Mathlib (the `Spectral` files concern spectral spaces/spectra).

annotations.label: Ordinal-indexed sequence

anchor.snippet: An ordinal-indexed sequence is a generalization of a sequence

annotations.note: `Ordinal` exists and ordinal-indexed families are used (e.g. `o.toType → α`), but there is no dedicated ordinal-indexed-sequence definition.

annotations.label: Lists

anchor.snippet: finite sequences are called lists

annotations.note: `List` is the core type of finite sequences, used throughout Mathlib.

annotations.label: Streams

anchor.snippet: Potentially infinite sequences are called streams

annotations.note: `Stream' α := ℕ → α` is Mathlib's stream of infinite sequences.

annotations.label: Strings

anchor.snippet: Finite sequences of characters or digits are called strings

annotations.note: `String` is the core type of finite character sequences.

annotations.label: Cantor space

anchor.snippet: is sometimes called the Cantor space

annotations.note: `cantorSetHomeomorphNatToBool : cantorSet ≃ₜ (ℕ → Bool)` identifies the space of binary sequences with the Cantor set.

annotations.label: Binary sequence representing a formal language

anchor.snippet: An infinite binary sequence can represent a formal language

annotations.note: `Language α := Set (List α)` is formalized, but the binary-sequence-as-characteristic-function correspondence is not a stated theorem.
